original_abstract:
Creating nanoparticles with uniform dimensions and spatially precise functionality remains a significant challenge. While living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has proven effective for synthesizing one-dimensional (1D) and 2D core–shell micelles through the seeded growth of amphiphiles, the process has historically been limited to a single core chemistry. This restriction has hindered the development of complex nanoparticles with segmented cores of varying compositions—structures essential for enabling selective chemical transformations or programmed responses to external stimuli.

In this study, we present a versatile seeded growth strategy that utilizes various combinations of crystallizable polylactone homopolymer/block copolymer blends to produce 2D platelet micelles with compositionally distinct, segmented cores. By precisely tuning the internal core chemistry, we demonstrate spatially selective hydrolytic degradation within the platelets. This ability to control the location and timing of degradation provides a framework for designing sophisticated, stimuli-responsive particles tailored for advanced applications in programmed release and targeted cargo delivery.
humanized_abstract:
Preparation of uniform nanoparticles with desired functionality in space is highly desired but remains highly challenging. Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has demonstrated the possibility of in situ seeded growth of amphiphiles towards forming uniform one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) core–shell micelles for specific applications. However, most reports on living CDSA are restricted to the use of a single type of core segment, whereas preparation of nanoparticles with segmented cores of different compositions for achieving desired chemical reactions or dual responsive behavior remains a highly unsolved challenge.

Previous seeded growth strategies afforded access to novel two dimensional platelet micelles composed of a variety of crystallizable polylactone homopolymer/block copolymer blends and compositionally distinct segmented cores. Here, through a nuanced control of the core chemistry of the 2D structure we have demonstrated the ability to catalyze hydrolytic degradation selectively in distinct domains within the elongated platelike morphology and over differing time frames, paving the way toward the fabrication of complex release materials or nano-vehicles, acting as stimuli responsive vectors for on demand delivery of payloads.

Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has demonstrated the possibility of in situ seeded growth of amphiphiles towards forming uniform one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) core–shell micelles for specific applications. However, most reports on living CDSA are restricted to the use of a single type of core segment, whereas preparation of nanoparticles with segmented cores of different compositions for achieving desired chemical reactions or dual responsive behavior remains a highly unsolved challenge.

Previous seeded growth strategies afforded access to novel two dimensional platelet micelles composed of a variety of crystallizable polylactone homopolymer/block copolymer blends and compositionally distinct segmented cores. document.input:
Creating nanoparticles with uniform dimensions and spatially precise functionality remains a significant challenge. While living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has proven effective for synthesizing one-dimensional (1D) and 2D core–shell micelles through the seeded growth of amphiphiles, the process has historically been limited to a single core chemistry.
